narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates an 84% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. This model looks at key indicators like academic performance, test scores, and background to forecast bar success, and unfortunately, several of your profile elements stood out as risks. Your LSAT score of 18, far below the typical 37, was the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, as lower scores like yours often signal challenges ahead. Next in influence were your low rankings in school grades—being in the bottom decile for both your first and third years, compared to average mid-level positions—further reinforcing the model's concern about consistent academic strength. Your low family income bracket also played a notable role in tipping the scales toward predicted failure, as students from similar backgrounds sometimes face extra hurdles. Your racial background as Asian, underrepresented in the dataset where most are White, contributed similarly, though less strongly. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.6, under the average 3.2, added to this picture of below-average preparation. In contrast, being male slightly nudged the prediction toward success, bucking a minor trend in the data. Your choice to study full-time aligned with the norm and had minimal impact. Overall, these academic and score-related factors ranked highest in driving the high failure odds, far outweighing your personal demographics. Compared to typical admitted students, your metrics are in the lower ranges across the board, which is why the model flagged this as a mismatch. To boost your future bar chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT for a higher score if possible, build stronger study habits to mimic top decile performers, and seek scholarships or support programs to offset income-related barriers. With targeted prep like bar-specific courses, many students turn similar profiles around—keep pushing, and consider reapplying after showing progress.
